sections.heading: Required Access
sections.body:
To deploy a federated database instance, you must have [Project Owner](<Project Owner>) access to the project.

> **Important:** Users with [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>) access must add themselves as
> a `Project Owner` to the project before deploying their federated database instance.

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body:
Before deploying your federated database instance in your AWS (Amazon Web Services) S3 (Simple Storage Service) bucket,
you have to complete the following steps:

- Create a [MongoDB Atlas](https://www.mongodb.com/cloud/atlas)
  account, if you do not have one already.

- [Install the AWS CLI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/userguide/cli-chap-install.html).

- [Configure the AWS CLI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/userguide/cli-chap-configure.html#cli-quick-configuration)
  to access your AWS (Amazon Web Services) account. Alternatively, you must have access to
  the AWS (Amazon Web Services) Management Console with permission to create [IAM roles](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles_create_for-user.html).

* *Optional.* [Set Up Unified AWS Access](atlas/security/set-up-unified-aws-access).

sections.heading: Procedure
sections.body:
To create a new Data Federation database using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas dataFederation create <name> [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas dataFederation create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dataFederation-create).

sections.heading: Required Access
sections.body:
To deploy a federated database instance, you must have [Project Owner](<Project Owner>) access to the project.

> **Important:** Users with [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>) access must add themselves as
> a `Project Owner` to the project before deploying their federated database instance.

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body:
Before deploying your federated database instance in your AWS (Amazon Web Services) S3 (Simple Storage Service) bucket,
you have to complete the following steps:

- Create a [MongoDB Atlas](https://www.mongodb.com/cloud/atlas)
  account, if you do not have one already.

- [Install the AWS CLI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/userguide/cli-chap-install.html).

- [Configure the AWS CLI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/userguide/cli-chap-configure.html#cli-quick-configuration)
  to access your AWS (Amazon Web Services) account. Alternatively, you must have access to
  the AWS (Amazon Web Services) Management Console with permission to create [IAM roles](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles_create_for-user.html).

* *Optional.* [Set Up Unified AWS Access](atlas/security/set-up-unified-aws-access).

sections.heading: Procedure
sections.body:
- Click the Create New Federated Database dropdown

- Select **Manual setup** from the menu

* Select AWS (Amazon Web Services) as your cloud provider

* Type in your federated database instance name

* Click Continue

IMPORTANT: Your federated database instance name defaults to `FederatedDatabaseInstance[n]`.
Once your federated database instance is created, you cannot rename it.

- For a visual interface, click Visual.

- For editing directly in the JSON (Javascript Object Notation) file, click JSON Editor.

Visual Editor

- Select the dataset for your federated database instance from the Data Sources
  section.

  Click Add Data Sources to select your data store.

- Specify your data store.

  Choose Amazon S3 to configure a federated database instance for data in AWS (Amazon Web Services)
  S3 (Simple Storage Service) buckets.

  Corresponds to [stores.\[n\].provider](datalakeconf-aws.stores.\[n].provider) JSON (Javascript Object Notation)
  configuration setting.

- Select an AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM role for Atlas.

  You can select an existing AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM role that Atlas is
  authorized for from the role selection dropdown list or choose
  Authorize an AWS IAM Role to authorize a new role.

  If you selected an existing role that Atlas is authorized for,
  proceed to the next step to list your AWS (Amazon Web Services) S3 (Simple Storage Service) buckets.

  If you are authorizing Atlas for an existing role or are creating
  a new role, complete the following steps before proceeding to the
  next step:

  - Select Authorize an AWS IAM Role to authorize a new
    role or select an existing role from the dropdown and click
    Next.

  - Use the AWS (Amazon Web Services) ARN (Amazon Resource Name) and unique
    External ID in the Add Atlas to the trust relationships
    of your AWS IAM role section to add Atlas to the trust
    relationships of an existing or new AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM role.

    In the Atlas UI, click and expand one of the following:

    - The Create New Role with the AWS CLI shows how to
      use the ARN (Amazon Resource Name) and the unique External ID to add Atlas to the
      trust relationships of a new AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM (Identity and Access Management) role. Follow the steps
      in the Atlas UI for creating a new role. To learn more, see
      [Create New Role with the AWS CLI](atlas/security/set-up-unified-aws-access).

      When authorizing a new role, if you quit the `Configure a New
      Data Lake` workflow:

      - Before validating the role, Atlas will not create the
        federated database instance. You can go to the Atlas Integrations
        page to [authorize](atlas/security/set-up-unified-aws-access) a new role. You can
        [resume](atlas/security/set-up-unified-aws-access)
        the workflow when you have the AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM role ARN (Amazon Resource Name).

      - After validating the role, Atlas will not create the
        federated database instance. However, the role is available in the role selection
        dropdown and can be used to create a federated database instance. You do not need
        to authorize the role again.

    - The Add Trust Relationships to an Existing Role
      shows how to use the ARN (Amazon Resource Name) and the unique External ID to add
      Atlas to the trust relationships of an existing AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM (Identity and Access Management)
      role. Follow the steps in the Atlas UI for adding Atlas
      to the trust relationship to an existing role. To learn more,
      see [Add Trust Relationships to an Existing Role](atlas/security/set-up-unified-aws-access) .

    IMPORTANT: If you modify your custom AWS (Amazon Web Services) role ARN (Amazon Resource Name) in
    the future, verify that the access policy for the role includes the
    appropriate access to the S3 (Simple Storage Service) resources for the federated database instance.

    For further information, see

    - [Set Up Unified AWS Access](atlas/security/set-up-unified-aws-access)

    - [Create a Cloud Provider Access Role](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-creategroupcloudprovideraccess)

  - Click Next.

- Enter the S3 bucket information.

  - Enter the name of your S3 bucket.

    Corresponds to [stores.\[n\].bucket](datalakeconf-aws.stores.\[n].bucket) JSON (Javascript Object Notation)
    configuration setting.

  - Specify whether the bucket is Read-only or both
    Read and write.

    Atlas can only query Read-only buckets; if you
    wish to query and save query results to your S3 (Simple Storage Service) bucket, choose
    Read and write. To save query results to your S3 (Simple Storage Service)
    bucket, the role policy that grants Atlas access to your AWS (Amazon Web Services)
    resources must include the `s3:PutObject` and
    `s3:DeleteObject` permissions in addition to the
    `s3:ListBucket`, `s3:GetObject`, `s3:GetObjectVersion`, and
    `s3:GetBucketLocation` permissions, which grant read access. See
    step 4 below to learn more about assigning access policy to your
    AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM (Identity and Access Management) role.

  - Select the region of the S3 (Simple Storage Service) bucket.

    Corresponds to [stores.\[n\].region](datalakeconf-aws.stores.\[n].region) JSON (Javascript Object Notation)
    configuration setting.

    IMPORTANT: You can't create a federated database instance if Atlas Data Federation is unable
    to retrieve the region of the specified S3 (Simple Storage Service) bucket.

  - **Optional**. Specify a prefix that Data Federation should use when
    searching the files in the S3 (Simple Storage Service) bucket. If omitted, Data Federation
    does a recursive search for all files from the root of the S3 (Simple Storage Service)
    bucket.

    Corresponds to [stores.\[n\].prefix](datalakeconf-aws.stores.\[n].prefix) JSON (Javascript Object Notation)
    configuration setting.

  - Click Next.

- Assign an access policy to your AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM role.

  - Follow the steps in the Atlas user interface to assign an
    access policy to your AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM role.

    Your role policy for read-only or read and write access should look similar to the following:

    Read-Only Access

    ```json
    {
      "Version": "2012-10-17",
      "Statement": [
        {
          "Effect": "Allow",
          "Action": [
            "s3:ListBucket",
            "s3:GetObject",
            "s3:GetObjectVersion",
            "s3:GetBucketLocation"
          ],
          "Resource": [
            <role arn>
          ]
        }
      ]
    }
    ```

    Read-Write Access

    ```json
    {
      "Version": "2012-10-17",
      "Statement": [
        {
          "Effect": "Allow",
          "Action": [
            "s3:ListBucket",
            "s3:GetObject",
            "s3:GetObjectVersion",
            "s3:GetBucketLocation",
            "s3:PutObject",
            "s3:DeleteObject"
          ],
          "Resource": [
            <role arn>
          ]
        }
      ]
    }
    ```

  - Click Next.

- Define the path structure for your files in the S3 (Simple Storage Service) bucket and click
  Next.

  For example:

  ```sh
  s3://<bucket-name>/<path>/<to>/<files>/<filename>.<file-extension>
  ```

  To add additional paths to data on your S3 (Simple Storage Service) bucket, click
  Add Data Source and enter the path. To learn more about
  paths, see [definitions](adf-path-syntax).

  Corresponds to
  [path](datalakeconf-aws.databases.\[n].collections.\[n].dataSources.\[n].path)
  JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration setting.

- Create the virtual databases, collections, and views and map the
  databases, collections, and views to your data store.

  - (Optional) Click the  for the:

    - Database to edit the database name. Defaults to `VirtualDatabase[n]`.

      Corresponds to `databases.[n].name` JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration
      setting.

    - Collection to edit the collection name. Defaults to
      `VirtualCollection[n]`.

      Corresponds to `databases.[n].collections.[n].name` JSON (Javascript Object Notation)
      configuration setting.

    - View to edit the view name.

    You can click:

    - Add Database to add databases and collections.

    -  associated with the database to add collections
      to the database.

    -  associated with the collection to add
      [views](manual/core/views) on the collection. To create a
      view, you must specify:

      - The name of the view.

      - The [pipeline](manual/core/aggregation-pipeline)
        to apply to the view.

        The view definition pipeline cannot include the `$out` or
        the `$merge` stage. If the view definition includes
        nested pipeline stages such as `$lookup` or `$facet`,
        this restriction applies to those nested pipelines as well.

      To learn more about views, see:

      - [Views](manual/core/views)

      - [db.createView](manual/reference/method/db.createView)

    -  associated with the database, collection, or
      view to remove it.

  - Select AWS S3 from the dropdown in the
    Data Sources section.

  - Drag and drop the data store to map with the collection.

    Corresponds to `databases.[n].collections.[n].dataSources`
    JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration setting.

JSON Editor

Your configuration for AWS (Amazon Web Services) S3 (Simple Storage Service) data store should look
similar to the following:

```json
{
  "stores" : [
    {
      "name" : "<string>",
      "provider": "<string>",
      "region" : "<string>",
      "bucket" : "<string>",
      "additionalStorageClasses" : ["<string>"],
      "prefix" : "<string>",
      "includeTags": <boolean>,
      "delimiter": "<string>",
      "public": <boolean>
    }
  ],
  "databases" : [
    {
      "name" : "<string>",
      "collections" : [
        {
          "name" : "<string>",
          "dataSources" : [
            {
              "storeName" : "<string>",
              "path" : "<string>",
              "defaultFormat" : "<string>",
              "provenanceFieldName": "<string>",
              "omitAttributes": true | false
            }
          ]
        }
      ],
      "maxWildcardCollections" : <integer>,
      "views" : [ 
        {
          "name" : "<string>", 
          "source" : "<string>", 
          "pipeline" : "<string>" 
        }
      ] 
    }
  ]
}

```

For more information on the configuration settings, see
[Storage Configuration](config-adf).

- Define your AWS S3 data store.

  Edit the JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration settings shown in the UI for
  `stores`. Your `stores` cofiguration setting should resemble the
  following:

  ```json
     "stores" : [
       {
         "name" : "<string>",
         "provider" : "<string>",
         "region" : "<string>",
         "bucket" : "<string>",
         "additionalStorageClasses" : ["<string>"],
         "prefix" : "<string>",
         "delimiter" : "<string>",
         "includeTags": <boolean>,
         "public": <boolean>
       }
     ]

  ```

  To learn more about these configuration settings, see
  [stores](adf-aws-stores-reference).

- Define your federated database instance virtual databases, collections, and views.

  Edit the JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration settings shown in the UI for
  `databases`. Your `databases` cofiguration setting should
  resemble the following:

  ```json
  "databases" : [
    {
      "name" : "<string>",
      "collections" : [
        {
          "name" : "<string>",
          "dataSources" : [
            {
              "storeName" : "<string>",
              "defaultFormat" : "<string>",
              "path" : "<string>",
              "provenanceFieldName": "<string>",
              "omitAttributes": <boolean>
            }
          ]
        }
      ], 
      "maxWildcardCollections" : <integer>,
      "views" : [
        {
          "name" : "<string>",
          "source" : "<string>",
          "pipeline" : "<string>"
        }
      ]
    }
  ]

  ```

  To learn more about these configuration settings, see
  [defaultFormat](adf-aws-databases-reference).

This page describes how to deploy a federated database instance for
accessing data in your Azure Blob Storage containers.

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body:
Before you begin, complete the following prerequisites:

- Create a [MongoDB Atlas](https://www.mongodb.com/cloud/atlas)
  account, if you don't have one already.

- [Install Azure PowerShell](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/azure/) or [Azure
  CLI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/). To learn more
  about these tools, see [Choose the right Azure command-line tool](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/choose-the-right-azure-command-line-tool).

- Configure [Azure PowerShell](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/azure/configure-global-settings)
  or [Azure CLI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/azure-cli-configuration).

- *Optional.* [Set Up and Manage Azure Service Principal Access](manage-azure-access).

sections.heading: Procedure
sections.body:
To create a new Data Federation database using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas dataFederation create <name> [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas dataFederation create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dataFederation-create).

This page describes how to deploy a federated database instance for
accessing data in your Azure Blob Storage containers.

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body:
Before you begin, complete the following prerequisites:

- Create a [MongoDB Atlas](https://www.mongodb.com/cloud/atlas)
  account, if you don't have one already.

- [Install Azure PowerShell](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/azure/) or [Azure
  CLI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/). To learn more
  about these tools, see [Choose the right Azure command-line tool](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/choose-the-right-azure-command-line-tool).

- Configure [Azure PowerShell](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/azure/configure-global-settings)
  or [Azure CLI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/azure-cli-configuration).

- *Optional.* [Set Up and Manage Azure Service Principal Access](manage-azure-access).

sections.heading: Procedure
sections.body:
- Click the Create New Federated Database dropdown.

- Select Manual Setup.

You can select AWS (Amazon Web Services), Azure (Microsoft Azure), or Google Cloud. Once your federated database instance is created, you
can't change the cloud provider where Atlas Data Federation processes your queries.

If you are configuring a federated database instance for data in an Azure Blob Storage container, you can't choose a cloud provider that is different from the cloud provider that is hosting your data. That is, you must choose Azure (Microsoft Azure).

Defaults to `FederatedDatabaseInstance[n]`. Once your federated database instance is
created, you can't change its name.

- For a guided experience, click Visual Editor.

- To edit the raw JSON (Javascript Object Notation), click JSON Editor.

Visual Editor

- Select the dataset for your federated database instance from the Data Sources section.

  Click Add Data Sources to select your data store.

- Specify your data store.

  Choose Azure to configure a federated database instance for data in Azure Blob Storage
  containers.

  Corresponds to [stores.\[n\].provider](datalakeconf-azure.stores.\[n].provider) JSON (Javascript Object Notation)
  configuration setting.

- Select an Azure (Microsoft Azure) Service Principal for Atlas.

  You can select an existing Azure (Microsoft Azure) Service Principal that Atlas is
  authorized for from the Service Principal dropdown or choose
  Authorize an Azure Service Principal to authorize a new
  service principal.

  If you selected an existing service principal that Atlas is
  authorized for, proceed to the next step.

  If you are authorizing Atlas for an existing service principal or
  are creating a new service principal, complete the following steps
  before proceeding to the next step:

  - Select Authorize an Azure Service Principal to
    authorize a new or existing service principal and click
    Continue.

    Use the Azure (Microsoft Azure) Service Principal *AppId* in the Add
    Atlas to your Azure Service Principal section to grant Atlas
    access through an existing or new Azure (Microsoft Azure) Service Principal.

  - Follow the steps in the PowerShell or
    AzureCLI tab in the Atlas UI to create a new
    service prinicpal or modify an existing service principal.

  - Enter the tenant ID and Service Principal ID in the respective
    fields after completing the steps in the Atlas UI.

  - Click Validate and Finish to proceed to the next step.

- Configure access to your Azure Blob Storage.

  In the Configure Azure Blob Storage page, you must
  configure Azure (Microsoft Azure) Storage Account credential delegation and Storage
  Container access. To do these:

  - Enter your Storage Account Resource ID in the Storage
    Account Credential Delegation field.

    To learn more, see [Get the resource ID for a storage account](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-account-get-info?tabs=portal).

  - Copy and run the command from the Storage Account
    Credential Delegation step in your Azure (Microsoft Azure) PowerShell
    to set up the credentials delegation.

  - Specify whether the storage container allows Read only
    or Read and write operations.

    Atlas can only query Read-only containers; if you
    wish to query and save query results to your Azure Blob Storage container,
    choose Read and write.

    Atlas Data Federation doesn't support writes to your Azure Blob Storage container using
    [$out](adf-out-stage).

  - Enter your Storage Container name.

  - Copy and run the command shown from the Storage
    Container Access step in your Azure (Microsoft Azure) PowerShell to set up blob
    container access.

  - Click Continue.

- Define the path structure for your files in the Azure Blob Storage container
  and click Next.

  - Enter the storage path to your Azure Blob Storage container.

    For example:

    ```sh
    https://<storage-account>.blob.core.windows.net/<container>/<file-name>
    ```

    To add additional paths to data on your Azure Blob Storage container, click
    Add Data Source and enter the path. To learn more about
    paths, see [definitions](adf-path-syntax).

    Corresponds to
    [databases.\[n\].collections.\[n\].dataSources.\[n\].path](datalakeconf-azure.databases.\[n].collections.\[n].dataSources.\[n].path)
    JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration setting.

  - **Optional**. Specify the partition fields that Data Federation should use
    when searching the files in the Azure Blob Storage container and the field
    value type.

    If omitted, Data Federation does a recursive search for all files from the
    root of the Azure Blob Storage container. If you don't select a specific field
    value type, Data Federation adds any value in that path in all queries.

    Corresponds to [stores.\[n\].prefix](datalakeconf-azure.stores.\[n].prefix) and
    [databases.\[n\].collections.\[n\].dataSources.\[n\].path](datalakeconf-azure.databases.\[n].collections.\[n].dataSources.\[n].path)
    JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration settings.

- Create the virtual databases, collections, and views and map the
  databases, collections, and views to your data store.

  - (Optional) Click the  for the:

    - Database to edit the database name. Defaults to `VirtualDatabase[n]`.

      Corresponds to `databases.[n].name` JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration
      setting.

    - Collection to edit the collection name. Defaults to
      `VirtualCollection[n]`.

      Corresponds to `databases.[n].collections.[n].name` JSON (Javascript Object Notation)
      configuration setting.

    - View to edit the view name.

    You can click:

    - Add Database to add databases and collections.

    -  associated with the database to add collections
      to the database.

    -  associated with the collection to add
      [views](manual/core/views) on the collection. To create a
      view, you must specify:

      - The name of the view.

      - The [pipeline](manual/core/aggregation-pipeline)
        to apply to the view.

        The view definition pipeline cannot include the `$out` or
        the `$merge` stage. If the view definition includes
        nested pipeline stages such as `$lookup` or `$facet`,
        this restriction applies to those nested pipelines as well.

      To learn more about views, see:

      - [Views](manual/core/views)

      - [db.createView](manual/reference/method/db.createView)

    -  associated with the database, collection, or
      view to remove it.

  - Select Azure Blob Storage from the dropdown in the
    Data Sources section.

  - Drag and drop the data store to map with the collection.

    Corresponds to `databases.[n].collections.[n].dataSources`
    JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration setting.

JSON Editor

Your configuration for Azure Blob Storage data store should look
similar to the following:

```json
{
    "stores" : [
      {
        "name" : "<string>",
        "provider": "<string>",
        "region" : "<string>",
        "serviceURL" : "<string>",
        "containerName" : "<string>",
        "delimiter" : "<string>",
        "prefix": "<string>",
        "public": <boolean>
      }
    ],
    "databases" : [
      {
        "name" : "<string>",
        "collections" : [
          {
            "name" : "<string>",
            "dataSources" : [
              {
                "storeName" : "<string>",
                "path" : "<string>",
                "defaultFormat" : "<string>",
                "provenanceFieldName": "<string>",
                "omitAttributes": <boolean>
              }
            ]
          }
        ],
        "maxWildcardCollections" : <integer>,
        "views" : [ 
          {
            "name" : "<string>", 
            "source" : "<string>", 
            "pipeline" : "<string>" 
          }
        ] 
      }
    ]
  }
  

```

For more information on the configuration settings, see
[Storage Configuration](config-adf).

- Define your Azure Blob Storage data store.

  Edit the JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration settings shown in the UI for
  `stores`. Your `stores` cofiguration setting should resemble the
  following:

  ```json
  "stores" : [
      {
        "name" : "<string>",
        "provider" : "<string>",
        "region" : "<string>",
        "serviceURL" : "<string>",
        "containerName" : "<string>",
        "delimiter": "<string",
        "prefix" : "<string>",
        "public": <boolean>
      }
    ]

  ```

  To learn more about these configuration settings, see
  [stores](adf-azure-stores-reference).

- Define your federated database instance virtual databases, collections, and views.

  Edit the JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration settings shown in the UI for
  `databases`. Your `databases` cofiguration setting should
  resemble the following:

  ```json
  "databases" : [
    {
      "name" : "<string>",
      "collections" : [
        {
          "name" : "<string>",
          "dataSources" : [
            {
              "storeName" : "<string>",
              "defaultFormat" : "<string>",
              "path" : "<string>",
              "provenanceFieldName": "<string>",
              "omitAttributes": <boolean>
            }
          ]
        }
      ], 
      "maxWildcardCollections" : <integer>,
      "views" : [
        {
          "name" : "<string>",
          "source" : "<string>",
          "pipeline" : "<string>"
        }
      ]
    }
  ]

  ```

  To learn more about these configuration settings, see
  [databases](adf-azure-databases-reference).

To add other data stores for federated queries, see:

- [Create Atlas Data Store From the UI](deploy-atlas-datastore)

- [Create HTTP or HTTPS Data Store From the UI](deploy-http-datastore)

- [Create Online Archive Data Store From the UI](deploy-oa-datastore)

> **Note:** You can't create an AWS (Amazon Web Services) data store for [federated queries](federated-queries) across different cloud providers.

This page describes how to deploy a federated database instance for
accessing data in your Google Cloud Storage buckets.

sections.heading: Required Access
sections.body:
To deploy a federated database instance, you must have [Project Owner](<Project Owner>) access to the project.

> **Important:** Users with [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>) access must add themselves as
> a `Project Owner` to the project before deploying their federated database instance.

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body:
Before you begin, you will need to:

- Create a [MongoDB Atlas](https://www.mongodb.com/cloud/atlas)
  account, if you do not have one already.

- [Install the gcloud CLI](https://cloud.google.com/sdk/docs/install).

- [Configure the gcloud CLI](https://cloud.google.com/sdk/docs/authorizing)
  to access your Google Cloud account. Alternatively, you must have access to
  the Google Cloud console with permission to create [IAM roles](https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/grant-role-console).

- *Optional.* [Set Up GCP Service Account Access](atlas/security/set-up-gcp-access).

sections.heading: Procedure
sections.body:
To create a new Data Federation database using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas dataFederation create <name> [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas dataFederation create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dataFederation-create).

This page describes how to deploy a federated database instance for
accessing data in your Google Cloud Storage buckets.

sections.heading: Required Access
sections.body:
To deploy a federated database instance, you must have [Project Owner](<Project Owner>) access to the project.

> **Important:** Users with [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>) access must add themselves as
> a `Project Owner` to the project before deploying their federated database instance.

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body:
Before you begin, you will need to:

- Create a [MongoDB Atlas](https://www.mongodb.com/cloud/atlas)
  account, if you do not have one already.

- [Install the gcloud CLI](https://cloud.google.com/sdk/docs/install).

- [Configure the gcloud CLI](https://cloud.google.com/sdk/docs/authorizing)
  to access your Google Cloud account. Alternatively, you must have access to
  the Google Cloud console with permission to create [IAM roles](https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/grant-role-console).

- *Optional.* [Set Up GCP Service Account Access](atlas/security/set-up-gcp-access).

sections.heading: Procedure
sections.body:
- Click the Create New Federated Database dropdown.

- Select Manual Setup.

You can select AWS (Amazon Web Services), Azure (Microsoft Azure), or Google Cloud. Once your federated database instance is created, you
can't change the cloud provider where Atlas Data Federation processes your queries.

You must configure your federated database instance on the same cloud provider as the data stores to which your federated database instance maps.

Defaults to `FederatedDatabaseInstance[n]`. Once your federated database instance is
created, you can't change its name.

- For a guided experience, click Visual Editor.

- To edit the raw JSON (Javascript Object Notation), click JSON Editor.

Visual Editor

- Select the dataset for your federated database instance from the Data Sources
  section.

  Click Add Data Sources to select your data store.

- Specify your data store.

  Choose Google Cloud Storage to configure a federated database instance for
  data in Google Cloud Storage buckets.

  Corresponds to `stores.[n].provider` JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration
  setting.

- Select a Google Cloud Service Account for Atlas.

  You can select an existing Google Cloud Service Account that Atlas
  is authorized for from the role selection dropdown list or choose
  Create a Google Service Account.

  If you selected an existing account that Atlas is authorized
  for, click Next and proceed to the next step to list
  your Google Cloud Storage buckets.

  If you are creating a new service account, select Create
  a Google Service Account and click Next.

  > **See Also:** 
  >
  > - [Set Up GCP Service Account Access](atlas/security/set-up-gcp-access).
  >
  > - [Create a Cloud Provider Access Role](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-creategroupcloudprovideraccess)

- In the Configure Google Cloud Storage modal, follow the
  provided instructions to configure the Google Cloud CLI, then click
  Next.

- Configure Google Cloud Storage.

  - Enter the name of your Google Cloud Storage bucket.

    Corresponds to the `stores.[n].bucket` JSON (Javascript Object Notation)
    configuration setting.

  - Specify whether the bucket is Read-only or both
    Read and write.

    Atlas can only query Read-only buckets; if you
    wish to query and save query results to your Google Cloud Storage bucket,
    choose Read and write.

  - Select the region of the Google Cloud Storage bucket.

    Corresponds to the `stores.[n].region` JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration
    setting.

    > **Note:** You can't create a federated database instance if Atlas Data Federation is unable to retrieve the
    > region of the specified Google Cloud Storage bucket.

  - Grant access to your Google Cloud project.

    - In the Google Cloud console for the project that hosts your
      Google Cloud Storage bucket, navigate to IAM and Admin, then
      navigate to IAM.

    - Click Grant Access. In the modal that appears, in
      the New principals field, enter the Google Cloud
      Service Account associated with your federated database instance.

    - To grant read-only access to the bucket, apply the
      `storage.viewer` role. To grant read-write access to the bucket,
      additionally apply the `storage.editor` role.

  - **Optional**. Specify a prefix that Data Federation should use when
    searching the files in the Google Cloud Storage bucket. If omitted, Data Federation
    does a recursive search for all files from the root of the
    Google Cloud Storage bucket.

    Corresponds to the `stores.[n].prefix` JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration
    setting.

  - Click Validate and finish.

- Define the path structure for your files in the Google Cloud Storage bucket and click
  Next.

  For example:

  ```sh
  https://storage.googleapis.com/<path>/<to>/<files>/<filename>.<file-extension>
  ```

  To add additional paths to data on your Google Cloud Storage bucket, click
  Add Data Source and enter the path. To learn more about
  paths, see [definitions](adf-path-syntax).

  Corresponds to the
  `databases.[n].collections.[n].dataSources.[n].path` JSON (Javascript Object Notation)
  configuration setting.

- Create the virtual databases, collections, and views and map the
  databases, collections, and views to your data store.

  - (Optional) Click the  for the:

    - Database to edit the database name. Defaults to `VirtualDatabase[n]`.

      Corresponds to `databases.[n].name` JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration
      setting.

    - Collection to edit the collection name. Defaults to
      `VirtualCollection[n]`.

      Corresponds to `databases.[n].collections.[n].name` JSON (Javascript Object Notation)
      configuration setting.

    - View to edit the view name.

    You can click:

    - Add Database to add databases and collections.

    -  associated with the database to add collections
      to the database.

    -  associated with the collection to add
      [views](manual/core/views) on the collection. To create a
      view, you must specify:

      - The name of the view.

      - The [pipeline](manual/core/aggregation-pipeline)
        to apply to the view.

        The view definition pipeline cannot include the `$out` or
        the `$merge` stage. If the view definition includes
        nested pipeline stages such as `$lookup` or `$facet`,
        this restriction applies to those nested pipelines as well.

      To learn more about views, see:

      - [Views](manual/core/views)

      - [db.createView](manual/reference/method/db.createView)

    -  associated with the database, collection, or
      view to remove it.

  - Select Google Cloud Storage from the dropdown in the
    Data Sources section.

  - Drag and drop the data store to map with the collection.

    Corresponds to `databases.[n].collections.[n].dataSources`
    JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration setting.

JSON Editor

Your configuration for Google Cloud Storage data store should look
similar to the following:

```json
{
  "stores" : [
    {
      "name" : "<string>",
      "provider" : "<string>",
      "region" : "<string>",
      "bucket" : "<string>",
      "prefix": "<string>",
      "delimiter": "<string>"
    }
  ],
  "databases" : [
    {
      "name" : "<string>",
      "collections" : [
	{
	  "name" : "<string>",
	  "dataSources" : [
	    {
	      "storeName" : "<string>",
	      "path" : "<string>",
	      "defaultFormat" : "<string>",
	      "provenanceFieldName": "<string>",
	      "omitAttributes": <boolean>
	    }
	  ]
	}
      ],
      "maxWildcardCollections" : <integer>,
      "views" : [ 
	{
	  "name" : "<string>", 
	  "source" : "<string>", 
	  "pipeline" : "<string>" 
	}
      ] 
    }
  ]
}
    

```

For more information on the configuration settings, see
[Storage Configuration](config-adf).

- Define your AWS S3 data store.

  Edit the JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration settings shown in the UI for
  `stores`. Your `stores` configuration setting should resemble the
  following:

  ```json
     "stores" : [
       {
         "name" : "<string>",
         "provider" : "<string>",
         "region" : "<string>",
         "bucket" : "<string>",
         "additionalStorageClasses" : ["<string>"],
         "prefix" : "<string>",
         "delimiter" : "<string>",
         "includeTags": <boolean>,
         "public": <boolean>
       }
     ]

  ```

  To learn more about these configuration settings, see
  [stores](adf-aws-stores-reference).

- Define your federated database instance virtual databases, collections, and views.

  Edit the JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration settings shown in the UI for
  `databases`. Your `databases` configuration setting should
  resemble the following:

  ```json
  "databases" : [
    {
      "name" : "<string>",
      "collections" : [
        {
          "name" : "<string>",
          "dataSources" : [
            {
              "storeName" : "<string>",
              "defaultFormat" : "<string>",
              "path" : "<string>",
              "provenanceFieldName": "<string>",
              "omitAttributes": <boolean>
            }
          ]
        }
      ], 
      "maxWildcardCollections" : <integer>,
      "views" : [
        {
          "name" : "<string>",
          "source" : "<string>",
          "pipeline" : "<string>"
        }
      ]
    }
  ]

  ```

  To learn more about these configuration settings, see
  [defaultFormat](adf-aws-databases-reference).

To add other data stores for federated queries, see:

- [Create Atlas Data Store From the UI](deploy-atlas-datastore)

- [Create HTTP or HTTPS Data Store From the UI](deploy-http-datastore)

- [Create Online Archive Data Store From the UI](deploy-oa-datastore)

> **Note:** You can't connect an Azure Blob Storage data store for [federated queries](federated-queries) across cloud providers.

This page describes how to deploy a federated database instance for
accessing data in an HTTP (HyperText Transport Protocol) data store.

sections.heading: Required Access
sections.body:
To deploy a federated database instance, you must have [Project Owner](<Project Owner>) access to the project.

> **Important:** Users with [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>) access must add themselves as
> a `Project Owner` to the project before deploying their federated database instance.

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body:
Before you begin, you will need to:

- Create a [MongoDB Atlas](https://www.mongodb.com/cloud/atlas)
  account, if you do not have one already.

- Format your data store using one of the [Supported Data Formats](adf-data-formats).

  > **Note:** If your file format is `CSV` or `TSV`, you must include a header
  > row in your data. See [CSV](adf-csv-tsv-data) for more
  > information.

- Make your data store accessible over the public internet.

> **Important:** 
>
> - If your HTTP (HyperText Transport Protocol) data store is not accessible over HTTPS (Secure HyperText Transport Protocol), you must
>   use the JSON Editor to configure your data store.
>   In your JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration, you must set the
>   [stores.\[n\].allowInsecure](datalakeconf-http.stores.\[n].allowInsecure) setting to `true`.
>
> - Atlas Data Federation does not support HTTP (HyperText Transport Protocol) data store URL (Uniform Resource Locator)s that require
>   authentication.

sections.heading: Procedure
sections.body:
To create a new Data Federation database using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas dataFederation create <name> [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas dataFederation create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dataFederation-create).

This page describes how to deploy a federated database instance for
accessing data in an HTTP (HyperText Transport Protocol) data store.

sections.heading: Required Access
sections.body:
To deploy a federated database instance, you must have [Project Owner](<Project Owner>) access to the project.

> **Important:** Users with [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>) access must add themselves as
> a `Project Owner` to the project before deploying their federated database instance.

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body:
Before you begin, you will need to:

- Create a [MongoDB Atlas](https://www.mongodb.com/cloud/atlas)
  account, if you do not have one already.

- Format your data store using one of the [Supported Data Formats](adf-data-formats).

  > **Note:** If your file format is `CSV` or `TSV`, you must include a header
  > row in your data. See [CSV](adf-csv-tsv-data) for more
  > information.

- Make your data store accessible over the public internet.

> **Important:** 
>
> - If your HTTP (HyperText Transport Protocol) data store is not accessible over HTTPS (Secure HyperText Transport Protocol), you must
>   use the JSON Editor to configure your data store.
>   In your JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration, you must set the
>   [stores.\[n\].allowInsecure](datalakeconf-http.stores.\[n].allowInsecure) setting to `true`.
>
> - Atlas Data Federation does not support HTTP (HyperText Transport Protocol) data store URL (Uniform Resource Locator)s that require
>   authentication.

sections.heading: Procedure
sections.body:
- Click the Create New Federated Database dropdown.

- Select Manual Setup.

You can select AWS (Amazon Web Services), Azure (Microsoft Azure), or Google Cloud. Once your federated database instance is created, you
can't change the cloud provider where Atlas Data Federation processes your queries.

Defaults to `FederatedDatabaseInstance[n]`. Once your federated database instance is
created, you can't change its name.

- For a guided experience, click Visual Editor.

- To edit the raw JSON (Javascript Object Notation), click JSON Editor.

Visual Editor

- Select the dataset for your federated database instance from the Data Sources
  section.

  Click Add Data Sources to select your data store.

- Specify your data store.

  Choose HTTP(S) to configure a federated database instance for data in publicly
  accessible HTTP (HyperText Transport Protocol) and HTTPS (Secure HyperText Transport Protocol) URL (Uniform Resource Locator)s.

  Corresponds to [stores.\[n\].provider](datalakeconf-http.stores.\[n].provider) JSON (Javascript Object Notation)
  configuration setting.

- Enter a name for your HTTP (HyperText Transport Protocol) data store into the HTTP(S)
  Store Name field.

  > **Note:** The data store's name must be unique within your federated database instance.

  Corresponds to [stores.\[n\].name](datalakeconf-http.stores.\[n].name) JSON (Javascript Object Notation)
  configuration setting.

- Enter the publicly accessible URL (Uniform Resource Locator) of the file where data is stored.

  Atlas Data Federation supports JSON (Javascript Object Notation), BSON (Binary Javascript Object Notation), CSV (Comma-Separated Values), TSV, Avro (gzipped or
  uncompressed), Parquet, and ORC file types.

  > **Tip:** Click Use Sample URL to add a sample HTTP (HyperText Transport Protocol)
  > data store.

  For each additional HTTP (HyperText Transport Protocol) data store that you want to add,
  click Add Another URL, then enter the HTTP (HyperText Transport Protocol) data
  store URLs.

  Corresponds to [stores.\[n\].urls](datalakeconf-http.stores.\[n].urls) JSON (Javascript Object Notation)
  configuration setting.

- Click Next to configure virtual databases and collections.

- Create the virtual databases, collections, and views and map the
  databases, collections, and views to your data store.

  - (Optional) Click the  for the:

    - Database to edit the database name. Defaults to `VirtualDatabase[n]`.

      Corresponds to `databases.[n].name` JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration
      setting.

    - Collection to edit the collection name. Defaults to
      `VirtualCollection[n]`.

      Corresponds to `databases.[n].collections.[n].name` JSON (Javascript Object Notation)
      configuration setting.

    - View to edit the view name.

    You can click:

    - Add Database to add databases and collections.

    -  associated with the database to add collections
      to the database.

    -  associated with the collection to add
      [views](manual/core/views) on the collection. To create a
      view, you must specify:

      - The name of the view.

      - The [pipeline](manual/core/aggregation-pipeline)
        to apply to the view.

        The view definition pipeline cannot include the `$out` or
        the `$merge` stage. If the view definition includes
        nested pipeline stages such as `$lookup` or `$facet`,
        this restriction applies to those nested pipelines as well.

      To learn more about views, see:

      - [Views](manual/core/views)

      - [db.createView](manual/reference/method/db.createView)

    -  associated with the database, collection, or
      view to remove it.

  - Select HTTP from the dropdown in the
    Data Sources section.

  - Drag and drop the data store to map with the collection.

    Corresponds to `databases.[n].collections.[n].dataSources`
    JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration setting.

JSON Editor

Your configuration for an HTTP (HyperText Transport Protocol) data store should
look similar to the following:

```json
{
  "stores" : [
    {
      "name" : "<string>",
      "provider": "<string>",
      "defaultFormat" : "<string>",
      "allowInsecure": <boolean>,
       "urls": ["<string>"]
    }
  ],
  "databases" : [
    {
      "name" : "<string>",
      "collections" : [
        {
          "name" : "<string>",
          "dataSources" : [
            {
              "storeName" : "<string>",
              "allowInsecure" : <boolean>,
              "urls" : ["<string>"],
              "defaultFormat" : "<string>",
              "provenanceFieldName": "<string>"
            }
          ]
        }
      ],
      "views" : [ 
        {
          "name" : "<string>", 
          "source" : "<string>", 
          "pipeline" : "<string>" 
        }
      ] 
    }
  ]
}

```

For more information on the configuration settings, see
[HTTP URL](adf-configuration-file-http).

- Define your HTTP (HyperText Transport Protocol) data store.

  Edit the JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration settings shown in the UI for
  `stores`. Your `stores` cofiguration setting should resemble the
  following:

  ```json
  "stores" : [
    {
      "name" : "<string>",
      "provider" : "<string>",
      "allowInsecure": <boolean>,
      "urls" : ["<string>"],
      "defaultFormat" : "<string>"
    }
  ]

  ```

  To learn more about these configuration settings, see
  [stores](adf-http-stores-reference).

- Define your federated database instance virtual databases, collections, and views.

  Edit the JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration settings shown in the UI for
  `databases`. Your `databases` cofiguration setting should
  resemble the following:

  ```json
  "databases" : [
    {
      "name" : "<string>",
      "collections" : [
        {
          "name" : "<string>",
          "dataSources" : [
            {
              "storeName" : "<string>",
              "allowInsecure" : <boolean>,
              "urls" : ["<string>"],
              "defaultFormat" : "<string>",
              "provenanceFieldName": "<string>"
            }
          ]
        }
      ]
    }
  ]

  ```

  To learn more about these configuration settings, see
  [databases](adf-http-databases-reference).

To add other data stores for federated queries, see:

- [Create AWS Data Store From the UI](deploy-s3) or
  [Create Azure Data Store From the UI](deploy-azure)

- [Create Atlas Data Store From the UI](deploy-atlas-datastore)

- [Create Online Archive Data Store From the UI](deploy-oa-datastore)

This page describes how to deploy a federated database instance for
accessing data in an Atlas cluster.

sections.heading: Required Access
sections.body:
To deploy a federated database instance, you must have [Project Owner](<Project Owner>) access to the project.

> **Important:** Users with [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>) access must add themselves as
> a `Project Owner` to the project before deploying their federated database instance.

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body:
Before you begin, you will need to:

- Create a [MongoDB Atlas](https://www.mongodb.com/cloud/atlas)
  account, if you do not have one already.

- Create an [Create a Cluster](create-new-cluster), if you do not
  have one already. Atlas Data Federation supports Atlas clusters
  deployed to AWS (Amazon Web Services), Azure (Microsoft Azure), or Google Cloud.

  > **Note:** To use your Atlas cluster as a data store, you must deploy it
  > to the same project as your federated database instance.

- Add data to at least one collection on your Atlas cluster if you
  have not already.

sections.heading: Procedure
sections.body:
To create a new Data Federation database using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas dataFederation create <name> [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas dataFederation create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dataFederation-create).

This page describes how to deploy a federated database instance for
accessing data in an Atlas cluster.

sections.heading: Required Access
sections.body:
To deploy a federated database instance, you must have [Project Owner](<Project Owner>) access to the project.

> **Important:** Users with [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>) access must add themselves as
> a `Project Owner` to the project before deploying their federated database instance.

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body:
Before you begin, you will need to:

- Create a [MongoDB Atlas](https://www.mongodb.com/cloud/atlas)
  account, if you do not have one already.

- Create an [Create a Cluster](create-new-cluster), if you do not
  have one already. Atlas Data Federation supports Atlas clusters
  deployed to AWS (Amazon Web Services), Azure (Microsoft Azure), or Google Cloud.

  > **Note:** To use your Atlas cluster as a data store, you must deploy it
  > to the same project as your federated database instance.

- Add data to at least one collection on your Atlas cluster if you
  have not already.

sections.heading: Procedure
sections.body:
- Click the Create New Federated Database dropdown.

- Select Manual Setup.

You can select AWS (Amazon Web Services), Azure (Microsoft Azure), or Google Cloud. Once your federated database instance is created, you
can't change the cloud provider where Atlas Data Federation processes your queries.

If you want to query data in an Atlas cluster, we recommend that you select that same cloud provider as the cloud provider for your cluster. To query data in object storage such as AWS (Amazon Web Services) S3 (Simple Storage Service) or Azure Blob Storage, you must select the same cloud for your federated database instance as the cloud for your object storage.

Defaults to `FederatedDatabaseInstance[n]`. Once your federated database instance is
created, you can't change its name.

- For a guided experience, click Visual Editor.

- To edit the raw JSON (Javascript Object Notation), click JSON Editor.

Visual Editor

- Select the dataset for your federated database instance from the  Data Sources section.

  Click Add Data Sources to select your data store.

- Specify your data source.

  - Choose Atlas Cluster to configure a federated database instance for data on
    your Atlas cluster.

    Corresponds to [stores.\[n\].provider](datalakeconf-atlas.stores.\[n].provider) JSON (Javascript Object Notation)
    configuration setting.

  - Select the Atlas cluster that you want to use as a data
    store in the Provide Namespaces in this
    project section.

    Corresponds to [stores.\[n\].clusterName](datalakeconf-atlas.stores.\[n].clusterName) JSON (Javascript Object Notation)
    configuration setting.

  - Expand the databases and select the collections that you
    want to add to your federated database instance.

    To filter the databases and collections, enter text into
    the Search database or collection field. The
    dialog box displays only databases and collections with names
    that match your search criteria.

    Corresponds to the
    [databases.\[n\].collections.\[n\].dataSources.\[n\].database](datalakeconf-atlas.databases.\[n].collections.\[n].dataSources.\[n].database)
    and [databases.\[n\].collections.\[n\].dataSources.\[n\].collection](datalakeconf-atlas.databases.\[n].collections.\[n].dataSources.\[n].collection)
    JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration settings.

  - *Optional*. Specify the Cluster Read Preference
    settings by expanding the section.

    Corresponds to [stores.\[n\].readPreference](datalakeconf-atlas.stores.\[n].readPreference).

    | Field Name           | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
    | -------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
    | Read Preference Mode | Specifies the replica set member to which you want to route the read requests. You can choose one of the following from the dropdown:primary - to route all read requests to the replica set primaryprimaryPreferred - to route all read requests the replica set primary and to secondary members only if primary is unavailablesecondary - to route all read requests to the secondary members of the replica setsecondaryPreferred - to route all read requests to the secondary members of the replica set and the primary on sharded clusters only if secondary members are unavailablenearest - to route all read requests to random eligible replica set member, irrespective of whether that member is a primary or secondaryIMPORTANT If you add an Atlas cluster as a store, the default value is secondary.If you don't set anything in your federated database instance storage configuration, the default value is nearest. To learn more, see Read preference mode.Corresponds to stores.\[n].readPreference.mode. |
    | TagSets              | Specifies the list of tags or tag specification documents that contain name and value pairs for the replica set member to which you want to route read requests. To learn more, see Read Preference Tag Sets.Corresponds to stores.\[n].readPreference.tagSets.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
    | Maxstaleness Seconds | Specifies the maximum replication lag, or "staleness", for reads from secondaries. To learn more, see Read Preference maxStalenessSeconds.Corresponds to stores.\[n].readPreference.maxStalenessSeconds.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         |

  - Click Next.

- Create the virtual databases, collections, and views and map the
  databases, collections, and views to your data store.

  - (Optional) Click the  for the:

    - Database to edit the database name. Defaults to `VirtualDatabase[n]`.

      Corresponds to `databases.[n].name` JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration
      setting.

    - Collection to edit the collection name. Defaults to
      `VirtualCollection[n]`.

      Corresponds to `databases.[n].collections.[n].name` JSON (Javascript Object Notation)
      configuration setting.

    - View to edit the view name.

    You can click:

    - Add Database to add databases and collections.

    -  associated with the database to add collections
      to the database.

    -  associated with the collection to add
      [views](manual/core/views) on the collection. To create a
      view, you must specify:

      - The name of the view.

      - The [pipeline](manual/core/aggregation-pipeline)
        to apply to the view.

        The view definition pipeline cannot include the `$out` or
        the `$merge` stage. If the view definition includes
        nested pipeline stages such as `$lookup` or `$facet`,
        this restriction applies to those nested pipelines as well.

      To learn more about views, see:

      - [Views](manual/core/views)

      - [db.createView](manual/reference/method/db.createView)

    -  associated with the database, collection, or
      view to remove it.

  - Select Atlas Cluster from the dropdown in the
    Data Sources section.

  - Drag and drop the data store to map with the collection.

    Corresponds to `databases.[n].collections.[n].dataSources`
    JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration setting.

JSON Editor

Your configuration for an Atlas cluster data store should
look similar to the following:

```json
{
  "stores" : [
    {
      "name" : "<string>",
      "provider": "<string>",
      "clusterName": "<string>", 
      "projectId": "<string>",
      "readPreference": {
        "mode": "<string>",
        "tagSets": [
          [{"name": "<string>", "value": "<string>"},...],
          ...
        ],
        "maxStalenessSeconds": <int>
      }
    }
  ],
  "databases" : [
    {
      "name" : "<string>",
      "collections" : [
        {
          "name" : "<string>",
          "dataSources" : [
            {
              "storeName" : "<string>",
              "database" : "<string>",
              "databaseRegex": "<string>",
              "collection" : "<string>",
              "collectionRegex" : "<string>",
              "provenanceFieldName": "<string>"
            }
          ]
        }
      ],
      "views" : [ 
        {
          "name" : "<string>", 
          "source" : "<string>", 
          "pipeline" : "<string>" 
        }
      ] 
    }
  ]
}

```

To learn more about these configuration settings, see
[Storage Configuration](config-adf).

- Define your Atlas data store.

  Edit the JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration settings shown in the UI for
  `stores`. Your `stores` cofiguration setting should resemble the
  following:

  ```json
  "stores" : [
    {
      "name" : "<string>",
      "provider" : "<string>",
      "clusterName" : "<string>",
      "projectId": "<string>"
      "readPreference": {
        "mode": "<string>",
        "tagSets": [
          [{"name": "<string>", "value": "<string>"},...],
          ...
        ],
        "maxStalenessSeconds": <int>
      },
      "readConcern": {
        "level": "<string>"
      }
    }
  ]

  ```

  To learn more about these configuration settings, see
  [stores](adf-atlas-stores-reference).

- Define your federated database instance virtual databases, collections, and views.

  Edit the JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration settings shown in the UI for
  `databases`. Your `databases` cofiguration setting should
  resemble the following:

  ```json
  "databases" : [
    {
      "name" : "<string>",
      "collections" : [
        {
          "name" : "<string>",
          "dataSources" : [
            {
              "storeName" : "<string>",
              "database" : "<string>",
              "databaseRegex": "<string>",
              "collection" : "<string>",
              "collectionRegex" : "<string>",
              "provenanceFieldName": "<string>" 
            }
          ]
        }
      ]
    }
  ]

  ```

  To learn more about these configuration settings, see
  [databases](adf-atlas-databases-reference).

To add other data stores for federated queries, see:

- [Create AWS Data Store From the UI](deploy-s3) or
  [Create Azure Data Store From the UI](deploy-azure)

- [Create HTTP or HTTPS Data Store From the UI](deploy-http-datastore)

- [Create Online Archive Data Store From the UI](deploy-oa-datastore)

This page describes how to deploy a federated database instance for accessing data in an
online archive.

sections.heading: Required Access
sections.body:
To deploy a federated database instance, you must have [Project Owner](<Project Owner>) access to the project.

> **Important:** Users with [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>) access must add themselves as
> a `Project Owner` to the project before deploying their federated database instance.

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body:
Before you begin, you will need:

- An Atlas online archive in the same project where you intend to
  create the federated database instance.

sections.heading: Select the Data Federation option on the left-hand navigation.
Create a federated database instance.
sections.body:
- Click the Create New Federated Database dropdown.

- Select Manual Setup.

sections.heading: Select the cloud provider where Atlas Data Federation will process your queries against your federated database instance.
sections.body:
You can select AWS (Amazon Web Services), Azure (Microsoft Azure), or Google Cloud. Once your federated database instance is created, you
can't change the cloud provider where Atlas Data Federation processes your queries.

We recommend that you select the same cloud provider as the cloud provider that is hosting your data.

sections.heading: Type a name for your federated database instance in the Federated Database Instance Name field and click Continue.
sections.body:
Defaults to `FederatedDatabaseInstance[n]`. Once your federated database instance is
created, you can't change its name.

sections.heading: Select the configuration method.
sections.body:
- For a guided experience, click Visual Editor.

- To edit the raw JSON (Javascript Object Notation), click JSON Editor.

sections.heading: Specify your Online Archive and configure virtual databases and virtual collections that map to your data store.
sections.body:
Visual Editor

- Select Atlas Online Archive from the Data Sources
  dropdown to configure a federated database instance for archived data.

  Corresponds to [stores.\[n\].provider](datalakeconf-adl.stores.\[n].provider)
  JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration setting.

- (Optional) Click the  for the:

  - Database to edit the database name. Defaults to
    `VirtualDatabase[n]`.

    Corresponds to [databases.\[n\].name](datalakeconf-adl.databases.\[n].name)
    JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration setting.

  - Collection to edit the collection name. Defaults to
    `VirtualCollection[n]`.

    Corresponds to [databases.\[n\].collections.\[n\].name](datalakeconf-adl.databases.\[n].collections.\[n].name)
    JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration setting.

  - View to edit the view name.

  You can click:

  - Add Database to add databases and
    collections.

  -  associated with the database to
    add collections to the database.

  -  associated with the collection to
    add [views](manual/core/views) on the
    collection. To create a view, you must specify:

    - The name of the view.

    - The [pipeline](manual/core/aggregation-pipeline)
      to apply to the view.

      > **Note:** The view definition pipeline can't include the
      > `$out` or the `$merge` stage. If the view
      > definition includes nested pipeline stages such
      > as `$lookup` or `$facet`, this restriction
      > applies to those nested pipelines as well.

      To learn more about views, see:

      - [Views](manual/core/views)

      - [db.createView](manual/reference/method/db.createView)

    -  associated with the database,
      collection, or view to remove it.

- Drag and drop the Atlas Online Archive to map
  with the collection.

  Corresponds to [databases.\[n\].collections.\[n\].dataSources](datalakeconf-adl.databases.\[n].collections.\[n].dataSources)
  JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration setting.

JSON Editor

Your configuration for online archive should look similar to the following:

```json
{
  "stores" : [
    {
      "name" : "<string>",
      "provider": "<string>",
      "region": "<string>"
    }
  ],
  "databases" : [
      {
        "name" : "<string>",
        "collections" : [
          {
            "name" : "<string>",
            "dataSources" : [
              {
                "storeName" : "<string>",
                "datasetName" : "<string>",
                "datasetPrefix": "<string>",
                "trimLevel": <int>,
                "provenanceFieldName": "<string>",
                "maxDatasets": <int>
              }
            ]
          }
        ],
        "views" : [ 
          {
            "name" : "<string>", 
            "source" : "<string>", 
            "pipeline" : "<string>" 
          }
        ] 
      }
  ]
}
  
```

To learn more about these configuration settings, see
[Online Archives](adf-configuration-file-adl).

- Define your dataset or online archive as a data store in
  your federated database instance storage configuration.

  Edit the JSON (Javascript Object Notation) configuration settings shown in the UI
  for `stores`. Your `stores` cofiguration setting
  should resemble the following:

  ```json
  "stores" : [
    {
      "name" : "<string>",
      "provider": "<string>",
      "region": "<string>"
    }
  ]

  ```

- Define virtual databases, collections, and views for
  your dataset or online archive in your federated database instance storage
  configuration.

  ```json
  "databases" : [
    {
      "name" : "<string>",
      "collections" : [
        {
          "name" : "<string>",
          "dataSources" : [
            {
              "storeName" : "<string>",
              "datasetName" : "<string>",
              "datasetPrefix": "<string>",
              "trimLevel": <int>,
              "provenanceFieldName": "<string>",
              "maxDatasets": <int>
            }
          ]
        }
      ],
      "views" : [ 
        {
          "name" : "<string>", 
          "source" : "<string>", 
          "pipeline" : "<string>" 
        }
      ] 
    }
  ]

  ```

sections.heading: Optional: Repeat steps in the Visual Editor or JSON (Javascript Object Notation) Editor tab above to define additional Online Archives.
sections.body:
To add other data stores for federated queries, see:

- [Create AWS Data Store From the UI](deploy-s3) or
  [Create Azure Data Store From the UI](deploy-azure)

- [Create HTTP or HTTPS Data Store From the UI](deploy-http-datastore)

- [Create Atlas Data Store From the UI](deploy-atlas-datastore)

sections.heading: Click Save to create the federated database instance with virtual databases, collections, and views mapped to your online archive.
